---
engine: markdown
title: "Sustainable Energy for All"
description: "Country-level energy data from the UN Sustainable Energy for All initiative, covering electricity access, energy efficiency, and renewable share from 1990 to 2010."
date: "2026-05-26"
order: 20260526
image: images/featured-light.png
image-alt: "Two stacked-area panels of renewable energy by source, 1990 to 2010. Vietnam's traditional biomass band shrinks and its renewable share falls from 76% to 35%, while Germany's modern bioenergy and wind and solar grow and its share rises from 2% to 11%."
categories: [2026]
---

```{typst}
//| include: false
//| file: _plot.typ
//| output-filename: featured.png
//| format: png
//| dpi: 300
//| width: 18cm
//| height: 9.45cm
```

```{typst}
//| label: fig-2026-05-26
//| echo: true
//| code-fold: true
//| code-summary: "See the code that generated this plot."
//| file: _plot.typ
//| output-filename: plot.svg
//| width: 18cm
//| height: 9.45cm
//| fig-alt: "{{< meta image-alt >}}"
//| fig-cap: "Renewable energy by source as a share of total final energy consumption, 1990 to 2010, for Vietnam and Germany."
```

Data: [TidyTuesday 2026-05-26](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/tree/main/data/2026/2026-05-26).

The Sustainable Energy for All dataset tracks each country's energy mix from 1990 to 2010.
A high renewable share is not always a sign of clean, modern energy.
Across countries in 2010, the renewable share correlates 0.88 with the share coming from traditional biomass, that is wood, charcoal, and dung burned for cooking and heating.

Vietnam shows what happens as a poor country grows richer.
Its renewable share falls from 76% to 35%, not because it abandons clean energy, but because fossil fuels expand faster than traditional biomass can be replaced.

Germany shows the opposite road.
Starting from almost nothing, it builds modern bioenergy and then wind and solar, lifting its renewable share from 2% to 11%.
The same headline number hides two very different stories.
